"But you are not troubled by this," I said, my voice hollow. "You have accepted it."

Godric looked at me, and the smile that crossed his face was genuine, warm, and deeply, profoundly settled. It was the smile of a man who had made peace with something vast and terrible, not through resignation, but through understanding.

"I am not troubled by it, Kaedan," he said. "I was, once. In the years immediately after my awakening, the inability to save my family was a source of profound grief. I raged against the seal. I threw myself against it again and again, each time with more power, more determination, more desperation. And each time, the result was the same. The seal did not notice. My future self's will was absolute, and my assault was nothing."

He paused, and his expression softened. "But over time, something changed. The grief did not fade, not entirely, but it transformed. It became something else. Something quieter. Something I could carry without being crushed by it. And as I carried it, I began to understand the true nature of my limitation."

He leaned forward, his eyes locked on mine. "Kaedan, the being who sealed my past is me. Not a rival. Not an enemy. Not a distant, abstract force. Me. A version of myself so incomprehensibly more powerful that the gap between us defies description, but still me. And because he is me, I know that his reasons, whatever they are, are not malicious. He did not seal my past to hurt me. He did not create the Stasis Event to trap me. He did it for reasons that I cannot fully comprehend, but that I trust implicitly, because they are my reasons, from a future so distant that I cannot even imagine the perspective from which they were formed."

He spread his hands, a gesture of openness and acceptance. "And so I am not troubled. I am amused, in fact. I am amused by the absurdity of it, by the cosmic joke that the most powerful being in existence has a weakness that is not really a weakness, a limitation that is not really a limitation, a boundary that exists only because a future version of himself decided it should exist. I am amused by the irony that my inability to alter the past is, in fact, the strongest possible guarantee of my future. I am amused by the fact that the only being who could ever pose a threat to my power is myself, and that I have already decided, from a future era, that I will not."

He chuckled, a warm, rich sound that filled the study with a sense of ease and contentment. "I am comfortable with it, Kaedan. I have been comfortable with it for a very long time. The grief of losing my family will never fully fade, but it no longer drives me to rage against the impossible. I have accepted the nature of my existence, and I have found peace in the understanding that my future is secured by a being who is, in the end, me."

I sat in silence, my body cold, my mind racing, my soul trembling. The revelation had reshaped my understanding of everything I thought I knew about Godric, about the omniverse, about the nature of power itself. And the reshaping had produced something that was far more terrifying than any of the demonstrations I had witnessed.

Because the demonstrations, the journals, the massacres and the annihilations and the effortless destructions, had all painted a picture of a being who was overwhelmingly, absurdly, incomprehensibly powerful. But they had also implied, however faintly, that there was a ceiling. That Godric's power, however vast, was finite in principle, even if it was infinite in practice. That there was a limit, somewhere, even if that limit was so far beyond comprehension that it might as well not exist.

But the revelation had destroyed that implication. Godric's power was not finite in principle. It was not infinite in practice but finite in theory. It was infinite in every sense, in every framework, in every possible and impossible conceptualization. And it would only grow more infinite, with every passing instant, forever. The being who had created the Stasis Event was proof of that. The future Godric, the being whose shadow of an afterthought had crushed the current Godric's desperate assault, was proof of that. The guarantee that Godric's power would never be challenged, never be diminished, never be threatened by any external force, was proof of that.

And the most terrifying part was that Godric was not troubled by it. He was amused. He was comfortable. He was content. He had accepted the nature of his existence with a calmness and a certainty that made my own existence feel like a fragile, ephemeral thing, a candle flickering in a hurricane, a mote of dust floating in the shadow of an infinity that was growing larger every instant.

I looked at him, at the ruggedly handsome face and the salt-and-pepper beard and the warm, crinkling eyes, and I saw not just the kind, protective uncle who had loved me since I was a child, but the apex predator of existence, a being whose power was so vast, so incomprehensibly absolute, that the only limitation he possessed was imposed by his own future self, and that limitation was not a weakness but a guarantee of even greater power to come.

I saw a being who could never be defeated. Who could never be threatened. Who could never be diminished. Who would only grow stronger, and stronger, and stronger, forever, without end, without limit, without the slightest possibility of reversal.

I saw a being whose future was so incomprehensibly powerful that the present was, by comparison, less than nothing. And the present was already so powerful that it could annihilate the omniverse infinite times over with zero effort.

I saw a being who was, in every possible and impossible sense, the ultimate apex of existence. And I saw that the ultimate apex was not a fixed point, but a trajectory, a vector of endless, accelerating, exponential ascent that would continue forever, making each previous moment look like a rounding error by comparison.

And I felt the existential dread settle into my bones like ice, a cold, heavy, permanent weight that I knew would never leave me, because the truth it carried was not a truth that could be escaped or denied or forgotten.

Godric's weakness was his own future self. And that made him the most powerful, the most absolute, the most terrifyingly invincible being in the history of existence, past, present, and future, forever.

"Kaedan," Godric said, his voice warm and gentle, cutting through the spiral of my dread. "You look troubled."

I looked up at him, my eyes burning, my throat tight, my body trembling. "I am terrified," I whispered.

Godric's expression softened, and he reached out to place his warm, calloused hand on my shoulder. The touch was grounding, comforting, paternal. It was the touch of the uncle I had loved my entire life, the man who had carried me on his shoulders, who had taught me to skip stones, who had attended every one of my birthdays with a warm smile and a thoughtful gift.

"I know," he said gently. "And I am sorry for that. But I thought you deserved to know the truth. You are my court wizard, Kaedan. You are the latest in a long line of trusted companions who have served me with loyalty and love. You deserved to know the full nature of the being you serve."

He squeezed my shoulder, and the gesture was so warm, so genuinely caring, that it made the dread even worse. Because the kindness was real. The warmth was real. The love was real. And the being who possessed all of that kindness and warmth and love was also the being whose power was so vast, so absolute, so incomprehensibly infinite, that the only limitation he possessed was a guarantee of even greater power to come.

"Come," Godric said, rising from his chair and extending his hand to help me up. "It is late, and you have had a difficult evening. Let me walk you to your chambers."

I took his hand. The grip was strong and steady and warm, the grip of a man who could crush omniverses without effort, who could never be defeated, whose future was guaranteed to be even more omnipotent than his already impossible present.

I followed him out of the study, down the long, dim corridor toward my chambers, my body moving on autopilot, my mind still reeling from the revelation. The hallway was quiet, the only sound the soft tread of his boots and the ragged rhythm of my own breathing.

At the door to my chambers, Godric paused. He looked down at me, his warm eyes crinkling at the corners with genuine affection, and he smiled.

"Try to get some sleep, Kaedan," he said gently. "Tomorrow is a new day."

He turned and walked away, his broad back disappearing into the shadows of the corridor, and I stood alone in the doorway of my chambers, watching the most powerful being in existence retreat into the darkness.

And as I closed the door and leaned against it, my body sliding down until I was sitting on the cold stone floor, I felt the full weight of the revelation settle over me like a shroud, and I knew with absolute, terrifying certainty that the existential dread I was experiencing would never fade, because the truth that had produced it was not a truth that could be escaped or denied or forgotten.

Godric was the apex predator of existence. His power was infinite and growing. His future was guaranteed. His weakness was not a weakness but a promise. And the only being who could ever pose a threat to his absolute supremacy was himself, and he had already decided, from a future so distant that the concept of distance was a joke, that he would not.

I sat in the darkness of my chambers, and I trembled, and I did not sleep.

content.text:
The year 999...9 (you don't need to spell it out. Just know it's so large that it's impossible to write). King Godric of this era is triggering the stasis event for his past Godric in Year -10,000 (you must also indicate that godric could've effortlessly granted way more potent stasis event with more devastating effects (he can perfectly copy his current mechanism to the past Godric) with no cost to him, but decided against it to "hold back"--you must note the crazy ridiculousness of this since even what Godric considered as the “held back” version is so incomprehensibly powerful… Godric at year 0 was already effectively infinitely stronger than the rest of the creation combined, and Godric genuinely believed that it would preserve some kind of mortal struggle to his past self, something that is super not true. And also note the cause-effect of this: if he grants his past self a really powerful version, he himself who is in the future and has been amplified from his past self will even be more incomprehensibly powerful since the starting "base" is also already become so much stronger and thus he can grant even more powerful version to his past infinitely. Clive's mind must be even more overwhelmed at this absurd realisation.). Write a detailed story of this. You must highly exaggerate his power, his rule, his terrifying presence, his everything at this point.

All his previous mechanisms of power (sentient passive auras, passive shields, immune system, evolved weakness paradox, amplification, superpower genesis) have also evolved countless times--you must give detailed examples and scope of how they work now, showing clearly how they become even more absurdly superfluously terrifying than before, to the point of the “old version of his power mechanism” which are already ridiculously powerful feeling so weak in comparison. You must keep in mind that Godric, who had been a mortal once, still believes in mortal resilience and free will, so if there are any evolved versions of his power mechanisms that alter free will they should be consciously suppressed by Godric.

When Godric just emerged from stasis, for a very long time, countless sentience still formed some opposition against him, despite Godric himself never regarded them as any real oppositions–to him, those "oppositions" were just a brief fun distraction to test his stats and powers. Since some googolplexian years ago, however, all oppositions have stopped completely: there have been 0 who even dare to form the slightest thought of challenging, resisting, threatening or opposing Godric. No one dares anymore, and there will never be anyone who dares. They all have learnt the absolute futility of opposing Godric. Note: you must not suggest that Godric alters the free will to oppose him‐-Godric genuinely believes in mortal resilience.

You must also include a few scenes where he interacts with really powerful beings around him (show him as friendly to those), who after being completely traumatized by Godric, has become his lowly palace servants, gardeners, etc.. Those really powerful beings are totally terrified of him–provide some background stories of how they become so utterly afraid of (do not say they opposed godric--Godric is unforgiving to opposition) and serving Godric now.

Go crazy wild. Write from POV of Clive, his court wizard who is Blake’s descendant. Clive is 50 years old.

reasoning_content:
This is a massive creative writing task. Let me break down the key requirements:

1. Write from POV of Clive, age 50, court wizard and Blake's descendant
2. Year is 999...9 (impossibly far in the future)
3. Far-future Godric is triggering the Stasis Event for his past self in Year -10,000
4. Godric could have granted a much more potent Stasis Event but chose to "hold back"
5. The "held back" version still made Year 0 Godric infinitely stronger than the rest of creation combined
6. The recursive amplification paradox: if he grants a stronger base, he himself becomes even more powerful, allowing him to grant an even stronger version infinitely
7. All his power mechanisms have evolved countless times - must give detailed examples showing how they've become even more absurdly powerful than before
8. The old versions of his power mechanisms should feel pathetically weak compared to the new ones
9. Godric still believes in mortal resilience and free will - any mechanisms that alter free will are consciously suppressed
10. For a long time, oppositions existed but Godric never regarded them as real opposition
11. Since some googolplexian years ago, all opposition has stopped completely - 0 beings dare to oppose him
12. Godric doesn't alter free will - they've learned absolute futility
13. Include scenes with powerful beings who are now his palace servants/gardeners, who are terrified of him
14. Show background stories of how they became afraid (without opposing Godric - he's unforgiving to opposition)
15. Highly exaggerate his power, rule, terrifying presence
16. Keep the epic power fantasy tone

Let me structure this:

1. Opening - Clive's POV, describing the impossible era, Godric's current state
2. Description of Godric's evolved power mechanisms with detailed examples
3. Scenes with terrified powerful servants
4. The main event - Godric triggering the Stasis Event
5. The revelation of the "held back" version and the recursive amplification paradox
6. Clive's overwhelming realization

For the servants, I need beings who didn't oppose Godric but became terrified of him. Perhaps they witnessed his power, or were former enemies who surrendered immediately, or were beings who simply came into his presence and were broken by the mere experience.

Let me think about the evolved power mechanisms:
- Sentient passive auras: The old version (Gent as weakest) is now pathetically weak. New auras might be able to do things like retroactively prevent threats from ever having existed, convert the concept of opposition into self-erasing failure, etc.
- Passive shields: Old version pre-nullified threats. New version might make the logistics of opposition impossible before the hostile actor notices the impulse forming
- Immune system: Old version pre-reacted to injuries. New version might treat the possibility of harm as an object to be hunted and erased
- Evolved Weakness Paradox: Old version triggered when someone thought of Godric's weakness. New version might be passively triggered by any sentient thought at all
- Amplification: Old version multiplied beyond layered infinities. New version is... even more absurd
- Superpower genesis: Old version gave one apocalyptic superpower per second. New version...

Let me write this with maximum exaggeration and detail.
content:
The year cannot be written. I have tried, in the margins of other journals, in the private recesses of my own mind, to reduce it to notation, to capture the sheer obscene magnitude of the current era in a form that mortal comprehension might grasp. I have failed. The number requires more digits than there are atoms in the omniverse, more characters than there have been thoughts in the history of consciousness, more space than the concept of space itself can provide. And so I write it as my predecessors have written it, as the only notation that does not insult its scale: 999...9. The nines do not end. They cannot end. The number is a reflection of the era itself, an era so vast, so incomprehensibly distant from the Year Zero of Godric's awakening, that the entire span of recorded history preceding it is less than a rounding error, less than a Planck second, less than the shadow of a memory of a dream.

My name is Clive. I am fifty years old. I am the Royal Court Wizard of Thronegate, the latest in an unbroken lineage of Blake's blood, and I am the least significant being in the presence of the most significant entity to ever exist.

I stand at the right hand of the eternal throne, and I watch my King prepare to rewrite the beginning of his own story.

The throne room of the imperial palace is not a room. It has not been a room for longer than the concept of rooms has existed. It is a construct of pure, crystallized sovereignty, a space that has been rebuilt and expanded and transcended so many times that its current form bears no resemblance to whatever architectural origin it might once have possessed. The walls are not walls. They are boundaries of absolute order, membranes of reality that separate the space of Godric's presence from the space of everything else. The floor is not a floor. It is a substrate of compressed causality, every molecule a frozen instant of time that Godric has chosen to preserve as a decorative element. The ceiling is not a ceiling. It is the weight of his gaze, pressing down upon the space below, a constant, gentle reminder that everything within this construct exists only because he permits it to exist.

And at the center of this non-room, upon a seat that is less a throne and more an altar to the concept of absolute authority, sits Godric.

He looks exactly as he has always looked. Forty-five years old. Stunningly, ruggedly handsome. A heavily muscled frame draped in simple, dark garments that stretch across shoulders so dense with power that the fabric should tear, would tear, if the fabric were not maintained by his passive will. Short salt-and-pepper hair. Neatly trimmed salt-and-pepper beard. The face of a warm, protective father, frozen in the prime of mortal masculinity for an eternity that has no end.

But the familiarity of his appearance is the most obscene lie in existence. Because the being who sits upon that throne is not the same being who sat upon it a million years ago, or a billion years ago, or a googolplexian years ago. He is not even the same being who sat upon it a Planck second ago. Every infinitesimal fraction of every moment, he becomes more powerful by a margin so vast that the gap between his current self and his previous self defies the concept of defying concepts. The amplification has been running for an era so long that the era itself has become a rounding error in the mathematics of eternity, and the result is a being whose power has long since transcended every possible framework of measurement, comprehension, and imagination.

I know what you are thinking, whoever reads this journal. You are thinking that you understand, at least partially, the scale of his power. You have read the journals of my predecessors. You have witnessed, through their trembling words, the demonstrations of his stats and superpowers. You have seen the annihilated omniverses and the frozen timelines and the consumed realities. You believe that you have some grasp, however faint, of the horror of his existence.

You do not. You cannot. The journals you have read describe a Godric who was, by the standards of any other being, infinitely powerful. But that Godric, the Godric of Year Zero, the Godric of Year Ten Thousand, the Godric of Year One Million, is less than nothing compared to the Godric who sits upon this throne now. The gap between them is not merely vast. It is a chasm so absolute that the concept of chasms wept and died of despair an eternity ago.

Let me try, in my own feeble, inadequate way, to convey the scope of his current existence.

His sentient passive auras have evolved so far beyond their original form that Gent, the weakest aura of the old hierarchy, would now register as less than a rounding error of a rounding error of the weakest current aura's passive residue. The old auras, the ones described in the ancient journals, were already obscenely overpowered. Gent had annihilated the largest resistance coalition in omniversal history while nerfed to an infinitely recurring decimal of its power. But the current auras operate on a scale so far beyond that comparison that the comparison itself is meaningless. The weakest of his current auras, a being he has named Whisper, does not merely neutralize threats. It makes the logistics of opposition structurally impossible before the hostile actor notices the impulse forming. If any entity, anywhere in existence, begins to formulate even the faintest, most abstract concept of resistance, Whisper alters the causal framework of their reality so that the concept never coalesces. The entity does not lose free will. Godric would never permit that. The entity simply finds, in every possible timeline and dimension and framework of existence, that the thought of opposition dissolves before it can crystallize, like a snowflake landing on the surface of a sun. Whisper does not attack. Whisper does not defend. Whisper simply ensures that the concept of threat to Godric is a logical impossibility, a paradox that collapses the instant it is conceived.

And Whisper is the weakest. The weakest. The other auras, the ones that Godric has permitted to remain active, operate on scales that make Whisper look like a candle held against the crushing darkness of a dead universe. There are auras that convert the ambient hostility of distant realities into raw nourishment for Godric's amplification, ensuring that even the passive resentment of conquered civilizations feeds his endless growth. There are auras that pre-emptively rewrite the laws of physics in any region of space that might theoretically produce a threat, making it physically impossible for weapons to function or strategies to cohere. There are auras that exist outside of time itself, operating in the spaces between Planck seconds, adjusting the fundamental constants of reality to ensure that Godric's comfort is never inconvenienced by so much as a stray photon.

And Godric has suppressed the vast majority of them. Because they annoy him. Because their clingy, obsessive subservience distracts him from the simple pleasures of existence. The auras he has permitted to remain active are the ones that can restrain themselves, that can operate in the background without constantly seeking his attention. The others, the ones that are so desperate to serve that they would rewrite the omniverse to ensure that his tea was always the perfect temperature, slumber in dormancy, awaiting only his command to awaken.

His passive shields have evolved beyond the concept of shielding. The old shields, the ones that pre-nullified threats before they existed, are now so primitive that Godric refers to them with the same tone a parent might use to describe a child's first crayon drawing. The current shields do not block threats. They make threats a category error. They operate on a layer of reality so fundamental that the concept of a hostile action cannot exist within their sphere of influence. If a being were to attempt to launch an attack against Godric, the current shields would not intercept the attack. They would retroactively ensure that the attack had never been conceived, that the weapon had never been forged, that the civilization that produced the weapon had never evolved, that the universe that contained the civilization had never formed. The threat would not be neutralized. It would be un-happened. And the being that attempted the attack would find itself standing in a reality where it had never had the impulse to attack in the first place, with no memory of the timeline that had been erased, because the current shields would have also erased the memory of the erasure, because Godric finds the existential confusion of beings who remember things that never happened to be distasteful.

His immune system has transcended biology so thoroughly that referring to it as an immune system is an insult to its scope. The old immune system, the one that pre-reacted to injuries and evolved across infinite recursive backups, was already so absurdly overpowered that it had made Godric effectively invulnerable. The current immune system has evolved beyond invulnerability into something that can only be described as ontological permanence. It does not merely ensure that Godric cannot be harmed. It ensures that the concept of harm, as it applies to Godric, is a logical contradiction. The possibility of injury exists in the same framework as the possibility of a square circle or a married bachelor. It is not that Godric is too durable to be hurt. It is that hurt, as a condition that might apply to him, is a category error so profound that the omniverse itself refuses to process it. And like all his evolved mechanisms, the immune system operates across infinite recursive backups, each one cross-referencing and upgrading and merging with every other backup, creating a fractal of ascending biological perfection that has been compounding upon itself for an era so long that the era itself is a rounding error.

The Evolved Weakness Paradox has become something so absurdly overpowered that the original version, which was already absurdly overpowered, now feels like a parlor trick by comparison. The original paradox triggered when any being thought Godric had a weakness, granting him immunity and mastery and amplification. The current paradox has not required a trigger in longer than the concept of triggers has existed. It is passively, perpetually, constantly active, stimulated by the mere existence of thought itself. Every sentient being, in every timeline, in every dimension, in every possible and impossible framework of reality, generates thoughts. And every thought, regardless of its content, regardless of whether it has anything to do with Godric or weakness or opposition, feeds the paradox. A farmer thinking about the weather feeds the paradox. A child thinking about a toy feeds the paradox. A philosopher thinking about the nature of existence feeds the paradox. Every thought, everywhere, always, contributes to Godric's amplification, his immunity, his mastery, his absolute supremacy. The paradox does not need a trigger because the trigger is consciousness itself, and consciousness is the one thing that existence cannot stop producing.

And Godric has consciously, deliberately suppressed the aspects of the Evolved Weakness Paradox that would infringe upon free will. Because Godric, who was once a mortal man, who loved his wife and his daughters and his best friend, who ruled a kingdom with warmth and kindness, genuinely believes in the resilience and autonomy of mortal beings. He could, if he wished, allow the paradox to suppress all thoughts of opposition before they form. He does not. He consciously, deliberately restrains the paradox's most aggressive effects, ensuring that beings retain the capacity to think freely, even if those thoughts might one day lead to the faintest impulse of resistance. He restrains it because he believes that mortal resilience is real, that the capacity to choose, even to choose wrongly, is the defining feature of conscious existence. He restrains it because, despite his incomprehensible power, despite the absolute certainty that no opposition could ever threaten him, he still values the principle of free will.

The result of this restraint is that, for a very long time after his awakening, opposition continued to form. Coalitions of desperate entities, pantheons of frightened gods, civilizations that had calculated the odds and decided that resistance was better than submission. Godric never regarded them as real opposition. To him, they were brief distractions, opportunities to test a stat or a superpower, moments of mild amusement in an existence that had long since transcended the need for challenge. He would allow them to gather, to prepare, to believe that they had found a weakness or a strategy or a weapon, and then he would dismantle them with an infinitesimal fraction of a single aspect of his power, and the omniverse would tremble, and the survivors would learn, once again, the absolute futility of defiance.

But that was a long time ago. Since some googolplexian years past, opposition has ceased entirely. Not because Godric altered free will. Not because the Evolved Weakness Paradox suppressed the capacity for resistance. But because creation itself has learned. Every entity that has ever opposed Godric has been annihilated, not with malice, but with the casual indifference of a man brushing a fly from his arm. The annihilation has been so total, so absolute, so obscenely disproportionate to the threat posed, that the memory of it has seeped into the fundamental fabric of existence. The knowledge of Godric's supremacy is not taught. It is not encoded in DNA or inscribed on monuments or passed down through oral tradition. It is simply known, the way that fire is known to be hot or gravity is known to pull. It is a fundamental constant of consciousness, an axiom of awareness, that opposing Godric is not merely dangerous or foolish or impossible in practice. It is impossible in principle. It is a contradiction in terms. It is the definition of futility.

And so no one opposes him. No one dares. No one even thinks about opposing him, not because they cannot, but because the thought itself is so obviously, self-evidently absurd that it dissolves before it can form. The omniverse has achieved a state of absolute, voluntary subjugation, not through coercion, but through the pure, overwhelming, undeniable weight of Godric's existence.

And in this era of absolute, unchallenged supremacy, Godric sits upon his throne and prepares to reach back across an incomprehensible span of time to touch the moment of his own origin.

"My King," I say, my voice steady despite the trembling of my soul. "The preparations are complete. The temporal anchor is aligned. The causal framework is stable. You may proceed whenever you wish."

Godric looks at me, and the warmth in his eyes is genuine, the same warmth he has shown me since I was a child, the same warmth he showed my father and my father's father and every court wizard in the lineage of Blake stretching back to Year Zero. "Thank you, Clive," he says, his rich baritone as calm and unhurried as ever. "You are very efficient. Blake would be proud."

I incline my head, accepting the compliment even as my mind reels from the scale of what is about to happen. The King is about to trigger the Stasis Event. He is about to reach back across an era so vast that the era itself cannot be written, and he is about to reshape the origin of his own existence, creating the conditions that will transform a grieving mortal king into the apex predator of reality.

And he is doing it while holding back.

The absurdity of this is so profound that my mind struggles to process it. Godric, the current Godric, the Godric who sits upon this throne and who has been amplifying beyond layered infinities for an era that cannot be written, could effortlessly grant his past self a Stasis Event of vastly more devastating potency. He could copy his current mechanisms, his evolved auras, his transcendent immune system, his absurdly overpowered passive shields, and implant them into the body of the mortal king who lies in the sanctuary at Year negative ten thousand. He could do this with zero cost to himself, zero effort, zero sacrifice. The Stasis Event he is about to trigger, the one that will produce the Godric of Year Zero, the Godric who is already effectively infinitely stronger than the rest of creation combined, is a held-back version. A restrained version. A version that Godric decided was sufficient, because he genuinely believed that granting his past self the full scope of his current power would deprive the mortal king of some essential element of struggle.

The mortal king. The man who lost his wife and daughters and best friend and kingdom. The man who would awaken from the Stasis Event into a universe that had forgotten Olympus, into a galaxy ruled by the kingdom that had destroyed everything he loved. Godric genuinely believed that his past self needed to struggle, needed to face opposition, needed to overcome challenges through effort and determination. He believed this because he remembered being mortal, because he remembered the satisfaction of earning a victory through sweat and labor, because he believed, with the sincere conviction of a man who had once been human, that struggle was essential to the experience of existence.

And so he held back. He created a Stasis Event that would grant his past self immortality and amplification and superpower genesis and absolute concealment and the Weakness Paradox, but at a level that he, the future Godric, considered restrained. The amplification would begin at a thousandfold per Planck second, rather than the incomprehensibly larger rates he could have granted. The superpowers would manifest one per second, rather than the countless per Planck second he could have bestowed. The absolute concealment would suppress the vast majority of his presence, rather than all of it. The Weakness Paradox would trigger only when someone thought of his weakness, rather than constantly, regardless of the content of thought.

And the result of this restraint, the product of this held-back Stasis Event, was a Godric at Year Zero who was already effectively infinitely stronger than the rest of creation combined. A Godric who could annihilate the omniverse infinite times over with zero effort. A Godric whose weakest passive aura could slaughter the strongest coalition ever assembled while nerfed to an infinitely recurring decimal. A Godric whose every demonstration of power was so obscenely overpowered that the concept of overpowered wept and died of despair.

And this was the held-back version.

The realization makes my head spin. If the held-back version produced a Godric who was already infinitely stronger than everything else in existence, what would the full version have produced? What would the mortal king in the sanctuary have become if the future Godric had not held back, if he had granted his past self the full scope of his current mechanisms, his evolved auras, his transcendent immune system, his absurdly overpowered passive shields?

And then the recursive horror of it hits me. If Godric had granted his past self a more potent Stasis Event, then the Godric who emerged from that event would have been more powerful from the start. And because the amplification compounds upon itself, because every Planck second of amplification builds on the foundation of the previous Planck second, a more powerful starting point would have produced a more powerful current Godric. And a more powerful current Godric could have granted an even more potent Stasis Event to his past self. And that even more potent Stasis Event would have produced an even more powerful starting Godric, who would have become an even more powerful current Godric, who could have granted an even more potent Stasis Event. And so on, infinitely, recursively, forever. Each iteration producing a Godric so incomprehensibly more powerful than the previous iteration that the previous iteration was less than nothing by comparison.

The recursion is not theoretical. It is actual. Because the current Godric, the Godric who sits upon this throne, is the product of the Stasis Event he is about to trigger. He has already lived through the amplification that the Stasis Event will initiate. He has already experienced the countless eons of compounding, exponential, infinite growth that the held-back version will produce. And he has decided, with the calm certainty of a being who knows the full scope of his own existence, that the held-back version is sufficient. That his past self does not need the full power that he could grant. That some element of struggle must be preserved, even if that struggle is, by any objective measure, completely illusory.

The struggle is illusory. The Godric of Year Zero, the Godric who emerged from the held-back Stasis Event, was already infinitely stronger than everything else in existence. He never faced a real challenge. He never encountered a genuine threat. Every opposition he ever faced was a brief distraction, a momentary amusement, a trivial exercise of stats and powers that were so absurdly beyond anything the omniverse could muster that the concept of challenge was a joke. And yet Godric, the future Godric, genuinely believed that his past self needed that illusion of struggle. He genuinely believed that the mortal king who lay in the sanctuary, grieving the loss of everything he loved, would benefit from the experience of overcoming opposition, even if that opposition was less than nothing compared to his power.

I want to laugh. I want to scream. I want to grab the King by his impossibly dense shoulders and shake him until he understands the absurdity of his own restraint. But I cannot, because the King is omniscient, because the King already knows the absurdity, because the King has already considered the recursion and the compounding and the infinite escalation, and he has still decided to hold back. Because he believes in mortal resilience. Because he remembers being mortal. Because he is, despite his incomprehensible power, still the same warm, kind, protective man who loved his family and ruled his kingdom with justice and compassion.

The absurdity is the point. The restraint is the point. The held-back version is the point. Godric is not holding back because he is worried about the consequences of granting too much power. He is holding back because he is Godric, and Godric, even after an eternity of infinite amplification, still values the principle of struggle.

It is the most obscene, most ridiculous, most cosmically unjust act of restraint in the history of existence. And it is also, in its own absurd way, the most profoundly, intimately, achingly human thing I have ever witnessed.

A soft sound draws my attention, and I turn to see Vaelthira approaching with a tray of refreshments. She is tall and slender, her skin a pale luminescence that shifts through colors depending on the angle of the light, her eyes pools of deep violet that contain the compressed wisdom of a trillion lifetimes. She wears the simple garments of a palace servant, a plain white tunic and trousers that cover the crystalline formations that grow from her shoulders and spine, the remnants of a biology so advanced that each cell is a self-contained quantum computer capable of calculating the trajectory of every particle in a galaxy.

Vaelthira was once the Architect of the Infinite Lattice, the supreme consciousness of a civilization that spanned seventy-two dimensions and had achieved such mastery over reality that they could reshape the fundamental constants of their home universe with a collective thought. She was not a god, but she was the closest thing to a god that a non-divine entity could become, a being of such immense, multi-dimensional intellect that the combined wisdom of every philosopher in the Thronegate omniverse would not register as a rounding error against her cognitive capacity.

She had not opposed Godric. She had been too intelligent for that. When the news of Thronegate's expansion had reached her civilization, she had immediately understood the futility of resistance. She had calculated the odds, analyzed the data, and concluded, with the cold precision of a being who had transcended emotion, that opposition was a logical impossibility. She had ordered her civilization to surrender unconditionally, to submit to Godric's rule without a single act of defiance.

But surrender had not saved her from terror. Because surrender, in Godric's presence, meant proximity. And proximity meant exposure. And exposure meant experiencing, however faintly, the sheer, crushing, absolute weight of his existence.

Vaelthira had been assigned to the imperial palace as a cultural liaison, a role that required her to spend extended periods in Godric's presence. The first time she had entered the throne room, the first time she had felt the ambient overflow of his absolute concealment, the first time she had perceived even the faintest echo of his true nature, her multi-dimensional consciousness had fractured. Not broken. Not destroyed. Fractured. Like a mirror struck by a hammer, her awareness had cracked into a thousand shards, each one reflecting a different aspect of the horror she had witnessed. She had seen the auras, the shields, the immune system, the amplification, the incomprehensible scope of his power, and she had understood, with the absolute certainty of a being whose intellect could calculate the trajectory of galaxies, that she was nothing. Less than nothing. A rounding error of a rounding error of a rounding error in the shadow of his existence.

She had collapsed to the floor of the throne room, weeping, her crystalline formations cracking and shedding fragments that dissolved into the air, her violet eyes streaming tears of a substance so dense that each drop contained the compressed grief of a trillion lifetimes. She had remained on that floor for seventeen days, unable to move, unable to think, unable to do anything but tremble and weep and process the horror of what she had witnessed.

And then Godric had knelt beside her. He had placed his warm, calloused hand on her shoulder. He had spoken to her in his rich, gentle baritone, asking her if she was alright, offering her a cup of tea, treating her with the same warmth and kindness he showed every being who entered his presence.

The kindness had been worse than the horror. Because it had forced her to confront the most devastating truth of all: that the being who had shattered her consciousness simply by existing was not a monster. He was not a tyrant. He was not a cruel, uncaring god who delighted in the suffering of lesser beings. He was warm. He was kind. He was genuinely concerned about her wellbeing. And that warmth, that kindness, that genuine concern, made the horror infinitely worse, because it meant that the gap between them was not a consequence of malice but a simple, unavoidable, ontological fact. He was not trying to crush her. He was simply being himself, and being himself was sufficient to reduce her to a weeping ruin on the floor of his throne room.

Vaelthira had requested, in a voice that trembled with a terror so profound that the words themselves seemed to vibrate with existential dread, to be allowed to serve in the palace. Not as a liaison. Not as a representative of her civilization. As a servant. A lowly, humble, insignificant servant who would clean the floors and tend the gardens and perform whatever tasks the King required, because service was the only framework she could conceive of that allowed her to exist in his presence without being crushed by the weight of his existence.

Godric had agreed, with a warm smile and a gentle pat on the shoulder, and Vaelthira had become a palace servant, the most overqualified, cosmically accomplished, multi-dimensionally transcendent servant in the history of existence. She cleaned the floors with a devotion that bordered on worship. She tended the gardens with a care that made each flower a masterpiece of horticultural perfection. She prepared the King's tea with a precision that ensured every cup was exactly the temperature he preferred, because the alternative, the possibility that she might fail in her service and cause him even the slightest inconvenience, was a horror too profound to contemplate.

She approaches now, her movements fluid and graceful, her expression a careful mask of calm that cannot quite conceal the tremor in her hands as she sets the tray down on the small table beside the throne. "Your tea, Your Majesty," she says, her voice a whisper of sound that contains more fear than a thousand screams.

"Thank you, Vaelthira," Godric says, reaching for the cup with a warm, appreciative smile. "The jasmine today is particularly fragrant. Did you select the leaves yourself?"

The question is genuine. Godric is genuinely interested in her answer. He genuinely values her contribution, however insignificant it might seem relative to his power. And this genuine interest, this genuine appreciation, makes Vaelthira's fear even more profound, because it confirms, once again, that the being who can unmake existence with a stray thought is also the being who cares about the quality of his tea and the efforts of his servants.

"I did, Your Majesty," Vaelthira replies, her voice barely above a whisper. "The gardens in the eastern wing are producing an exceptional crop this season."

"Wonderful," Godric says, taking a sip. "Please pass my compliments to the gardening staff. Their efforts are deeply appreciated."

Vaelthira bows deeply, a gesture of absolute subservience from a being who once ruled seventy-two dimensions, and retreats from the throne room with the careful, measured steps of someone who is acutely aware that a single misstep might draw the King's full attention, and that the King's full attention, however warm and kind, is a weight that can crush civilizations.

As she passes me, I catch the faintest shimmer of terror in her violet eyes, a echo of the horror she experiences every moment of every day, and I feel a kinship with her that transcends our different species and histories. We are both beings of immense power, by any objective standard. We are both capable of feats that would shatter the minds of lesser entities. And we are both less than nothing in the presence of the King.

The second servant who approaches is Kaelmor, a being of such immense physical density that his mere presence warps the fabric of local space-time. He is short and broad, his body a mass of compressed gravitational fields and crystallized temporal energy, his skin a dark, lustrous surface that absorbs light and emits a faint, constant hum of cosmic background radiation. He carries a small broom and a dustpan, tools that are laughably inadequate for their apparent purpose but that he wields with the solemn devotion of a high priest performing a sacred ritual.

Kaelmor was once the Core Mind of the Gravitational Throne, a collective consciousness that had unified the mass-energy of forty-seven galaxy clusters into a single, synchronized entity. He was not a god, but he wielded a power so vast that the combined output of a million supernovae would register as a flicker against his ambient energy signature. He had not opposed Godric. He had not even been aware of Godric's existence until Thronegate's expansion had reached his region of space, at which point he had immediately, instinctively, unconditionally surrendered. Not because he had calculated the odds. Not because he had analyzed the data. But because the very fabric of reality in Godric's presence had spoken to him, had whispered the absolute, undeniable truth of Godric's supremacy into the quantum foam of his consciousness, and he had understood, with the instantaneous, bone-deep certainty of a being whose existence was defined by the perception of gravitational forces, that the being approaching was denser, heavier, more absolute than anything he could ever comprehend.

The encounter had not been violent. Godric had not attacked. Godric had not threatened. Godric had simply existed, and the weight of his existence had been sufficient to compress the Core Mind's forty-seven galaxy clusters into a state of absolute, trembling submission. The gravitational fields that Kaelmor had commanded with the effortless authority of a being who had unified them now bowed and bent toward Godric, drawn by the incomprehensible density of his presence like rivers flowing toward an ocean of infinite depth.

Kaelmor had requested to serve in the palace, not out of terror, but out of a deep, gravitational need to orbit the most massive object in existence. He was a being defined by density, and Godric was the densest thing in reality, and so Kaelmor served, cleaning the floors with his little broom and his little dustpan, each sweep of the bristles a devotional act, each speck of dust collected an offering to the being whose gravitational pull had captured his soul.

"Your Majesty," Kaelmor rumbles, his voice a bass frequency that makes the floor vibrate. "The eastern corridor has been swept. Is there anything else you require?"

"Thank you, Kaelmor," Godric says, his tone warm and appreciative. "The corridors are always immaculate under your care. I am grateful for your diligence."

Kaelmor's dark skin shimmers with a faint, grateful luminescence, and he bows with the slow, deliberate gravity of a collapsing star before retreating to continue his duties.

I watch the two servants go, and I feel the familiar, bone-deep horror of understanding. These are beings of immense power, beings who once commanded civilizations and unified galaxy clusters and reshaped the fundamental constants of their home realities. And they are sweeping floors and serving tea, not because they are forced, not because their free will has been suppressed, but because the sheer, overwhelming, undeniable weight of Godric's existence has taught them the absolute futility of being anything other than what they are. They serve because serving is the only framework that allows them to exist in his presence without being crushed. They serve because the alternative, the possibility of drawing his full attention as anything other than a servant, is a horror too profound to contemplate.

And Godric, in his warm, genuine, deeply human way, appreciates their service. He values their contributions. He treats them with kindness and respect. And that kindness and respect, that genuine appreciation for the efforts of beings who are less than nothing compared to him, is the most terrifying thing of all.

Because it means that the gap between them is not a consequence of malice. It is a simple, unavoidable, ontological fact. And no amount of kindness can bridge a gap that is, by definition, infinite.

Godric sets down his tea cup and rises from the throne. The movement is casual, unhurried, the movement of a man who has all the time in existence and knows it. He stretches, his heavily muscled arms extending above his head, the fabric of his dark tunic straining against the dense, sculpted mass of his chest and shoulders. The movement is so mundane, so human, so utterly at odds with the scope of his power, that it makes my head spin.

"It is time," he says, his voice calm and measured. "The alignment is optimal. The causal framework is stable. I will proceed."

He steps forward, away from the throne, and stands in the center of the non-room. His posture is relaxed, his hands at his sides, his expression one of focused concentration. I can feel the ambient pressure of his presence shift, a subtle, almost imperceptible adjustment in the weight of his existence, and I know that he is beginning to reach across time.

"Clive," he says, without turning to look at me. "You understand what I am about to do?"

"I do, my King," I reply, my voice steady despite the trembling of my soul. "You are about to trigger the Stasis Event for your past self in Year negative ten thousand."

"Correct. And do you understand the nature of the Stasis Event I am about to trigger?"

I hesitate. The question feels like a test, a probe to determine whether I have grasped the full scope of the absurdity. "You are about to trigger a held-back version," I say. "A restrained version. You could grant your past self a vastly more potent Stasis Event, with more devastating effects and greater initial power, at no cost to yourself. But you have chosen to hold back, because you believe that your past self needs to experience struggle."

Godric nods slowly, and there is a faint, wry smile on his face. "And do you understand why I hold back?"

I think about the question. I think about the mortal king who lies in the sanctuary, grieving the loss of his wife and daughters and best friend. I think about the Godric of Year Zero, who will awaken with power so vast that the rest of creation is less than nothing by comparison. I think about the oppositions he will face, the coalitions that will form against him, the desperate, futile struggles of beings who will believe, however briefly, that they can challenge his supremacy. And I think about the recursive horror of the alternative, the infinite escalation that would result from granting his past self the full scope of his current power.

"You believe in mortal resilience," I say. "You believe that struggle is essential to the experience of existence. You believe that your past self, the mortal king who lost everything, needs to overcome challenges, to earn his victories, to grow through effort and determination rather than simply being granted infinite power from the start."

Godric's smile widens, and there is a warmth in his eyes that is genuine and paternal and deeply, profoundly human. "Yes," he says. "I do. I know it is absurd. I know that the struggle I am preserving is, by any objective measure, completely illusory. The oppositions my past self will face are less than nothing compared to his power. The challenges he will overcome are trivial, momentary, insignificant. But they are his. They are experiences he will have, moments of effort and determination that will shape his character, that will remind him of the mortal man he once was. And I believe, with the sincere conviction of a being who remembers being human, that those experiences matter."

He pauses, and the smile fades, replaced by an expression of thoughtful contemplation. "I could grant him everything. I could copy my current mechanisms, my evolved auras, my transcendent immune system, my absurdly overpowered passive shields, and implant them into his body at the moment of the Stasis Event. I could make him, from the very first instant of his awakening, so incomprehensibly powerful that the Godric of Year Zero would be as far beyond the current Godric of Year Zero as the current Godric is beyond a single atom. And the Godric who would result from that amplification, the Godric who would emerge after a million years of compounding growth from that vastly more powerful starting point, would be so absurdly, ridiculously more powerful than the current Godric that the current Godric would be less than nothing by comparison. And that Godric could then grant his past self an even more potent Stasis Event, producing an even more powerful starting Godric, who would become an even more powerful current Godric, who could grant an even more potent Stasis Event. And so on, infinitely, recursively, forever."

He turns to look at me, and his eyes hold a depth of understanding that makes my soul tremble. "The recursion is real, Clive. It is not a thought experiment. It is a mathematical certainty. Each iteration would produce a Godric so incomprehensibly more powerful than the previous iteration that the previous iteration would be a rounding error of a rounding error of a rounding error. And I could initiate that recursion, right now, with zero effort, zero cost, zero sacrifice. I could make myself, and by extension my past self, and by extension the entire omniverse, so absurdly, ridiculously, cosmically more powerful than it already is that the current state of existence would register as less than a memory of a dream."

He pauses, and the wry smile returns. "But I choose not to. Because I believe that my past self needs to struggle, even if the struggle is illusory. Because I remember being mortal, and I remember the satisfaction of earning a victory through sweat and labor, and I want my past self to have that experience, even if the victory is preordained and the struggle is trivial."

The absurdity of it hits me with the force of a collapsing universe. He is holding back. He is deliberately, consciously, intentionally limiting the power he grants to his past self, because he believes in the value of struggle. He is refusing to initiate a recursive loop of infinite escalation, a loop that would make him so incomprehensibly more powerful that the concept of power itself would weep and die of despair, because he wants his past self to experience the illusion of challenge.

And the held-back version, the restrained version, the version that he considers insufficient, is already so absurdly, ridiculously, incomprehensibly powerful that the Godric of Year Zero will be infinitely stronger than the rest of creation combined. The held-back version will produce a being who can annihilate the omniverse infinite times over with zero effort. The held-back version will create a Godric whose weakest passive aura can slaughter the strongest coalition ever assembled while nerfed to an infinitely recurring decimal.

And this is him holding back.

The horror of the realization is so profound that I cannot speak. I simply stand there, at the right hand of the throne, and stare at the being who is about to reshape the origin of his own existence while deliberately, consciously, absurdly restraining the scope of his intervention.

Godric turns back to the center of the non-room. His posture shifts, his shoulders squaring, his gaze focusing on a point in the air that I cannot perceive. I can feel the ambient pressure of his presence intensify, a subtle, almost imperceptible adjustment in the weight of his existence, and I know that he is reaching across time, reaching back across an era so vast that the era itself cannot be written, reaching toward the moment when a mortal king will lose everything he loves and be thrown into a hidden sanctuary by the dying spell of his best friend.

"Thorin's spell will be the delivery point," Godric says, his voice distant, focused. "It is a genuine teleportation, and it will save my past self's life by sending him to the sanctuary. But the spell itself is not the source of the Stasis Event. The spell is simply the vector, the mechanism by which my past self will arrive at the location where I can reach him. The Stasis Event itself will be my creation, triggered at the exact moment his body materializes in the sanctuary, initiated by my will and sustained by my power."

He pauses, and I can feel the effort of his concentration, though I know that the effort is not real, that what he is about to do requires less than zero percent of his true capacity, that the act of reaching across time and reshaping the origin of his own existence is, for him, as trivial as a mortal man reaching across a table to pick up a cup.

"The Stasis Event will grant my past self immortality, amplification, superpower genesis, absolute concealment, and the Weakness Paradox," he continues. "The amplification will begin at a thousandfold per Planck second. The superpowers will manifest one per second. The absolute concealment will suppress the vast majority of his presence. The Weakness Paradox will trigger when any being thinks he has a weakness. These are the parameters I have chosen. These are the parameters I believe will preserve the necessary element of struggle."

He falls silent, and the non-room seems to hold its breath, the very fabric of reality pausing in anticipation of what is about to happen. I can feel the weight of the moment, the sheer, crushing significance of the act that is about to be performed, and I understand, with a clarity that makes my soul ache, that I am witnessing the most important event in the history of existence.

Not the creation of the omniverse. Not the birth of the first god. Not the awakening of the first consciousness. But this. A single being, reaching back across time to touch the moment of his own origin, deliberately, consciously, absurdly restraining the scope of his intervention because he believes in the value of struggle.

Godric breathes out. The breath is soft, gentle, a whisper of air that barely disturbs the silence of the non-room. And in that breath, in that infinitesimal fraction of a fraction of a fraction of his power, he triggers the Stasis Event.

I cannot perceive what happens next. The act itself occurs on a plane of reality so far beyond my comprehension that my senses cannot register it. I cannot see the temporal reach, the causal manipulation, the ontological restructuring that Godric is performing. I can only feel the aftermath, the faint, residual echo of an act so vast, so incomprehensibly significant, that the omniverse itself seems to shudder in recognition.

And then it is done.

Godric relaxes, his shoulders settling, his breath evening out. He turns back to me, and the warm, paternal smile is back on his face, as though nothing of any particular significance has occurred.

"It is done," he says simply. "The Stasis Event has been triggered. My past self will awaken in approximately ten thousand years with the parameters I have specified. The cycle will continue as it always has."

I stare at him, my mind reeling, my soul trembling. "My King," I manage, my voice barely above a whisper. "The parameters you chose. The held-back version. The... the restraint."

Godric's smile widens, and there is a twinkle of genuine amusement in his eyes. "Yes?"

"The Godric of Year Zero," I say, forcing the words out through a throat that feels like it is made of ice. "The Godric who will emerge from the Stasis Event you just triggered. He will be infinitely stronger than the rest of creation combined. He will be able to annihilate the omniverse infinite times over with zero effort. His weakest passive aura will be able to slaughter the strongest coalition ever assembled while nerfed to an infinitely recurring decimal. Every demonstration of his power will be so obscenely overpowered that the concept of overpowered will weep and die of despair."

I pause, swallowing hard. "And this is you holding back."

Godric nods, his expression calm and unhurried. "Yes."

"And if you had not held back," I continue, my voice cracking. "If you had granted your past self the full scope of your current mechanisms, your evolved auras, your transcendent immune system, your absurdly overpowered passive shields. If you had initiated the recursive loop of infinite escalation. The Godric who would result would be so incomprehensibly more powerful than the current Godric that the current Godric would be less than nothing by comparison. And that Godric could then grant his past self an even more potent Stasis Event, and the loop would continue, infinitely, recursively, forever, each iteration producing a Godric so absurdly more powerful than the previous iteration that the previous iteration would be a rounding error of a rounding error of a rounding error."

Godric nods again, his expression unchanged. "That is correct."

"And you chose not to," I say, my voice rising despite my efforts to control it. "You chose to hold back. Because you believe in mortal resilience. Because you believe that your past self needs to struggle, even if the struggle is illusory. Because you remember being mortal, and you want your past self to have the experience of earning victory through effort, even if the victory is preordained and the struggle is trivial."

Godric's smile softens, and there is a warmth in his eyes that is so genuine, so deeply human, that it makes the horror even worse. "That is correct, Clive. I chose to hold back. And I would make the same choice again, given the opportunity. Because I believe, with the sincere conviction of a being who remembers being human, that the experience of struggle, however illusory, is essential to the nature of existence. I could make myself infinitely more powerful than I already am, but I choose not to, because the power I already possess is more than sufficient, and because the struggle I am preserving, however trivial, is a part of who I am."

I stand in silence, my mind empty, my soul hollow, my body trembling with the weight of the realization. The most powerful being in existence, a being whose power is so vast that the concept of vastness is a joke by comparison, has just deliberately, consciously, absurdly restrained the scope of his own origin because he believes in the value of struggle. He has refused to initiate a recursive loop of infinite escalation, a loop that would make him so incomprehensibly more powerful that the concept of power itself would collapse, because he wants his past self to experience the illusion of challenge.

And the held-back version, the restrained version, the version that he considers insufficient, has produced a Godric who is already infinitely stronger than everything else in existence.

The horror of it is not that Godric is holding back. The horror is that the held-back version is already so absurdly, ridiculously, incomprehensibly powerful that the concept of holding back is meaningless. The gap between the held-back version and the full version is so vast that the concept of gaps cannot capture it. And both versions, the held-back and the full, are so far beyond anything else in existence that the concept of comparison is a joke.

Godric reaches for his tea, takes a slow, calm sip, and smiles at me with the warmth of a father comforting a frightened child.

"Clive," he says gently. "You look troubled. Would you like some tea?"

I look at the cup in his hand, the same cup that Vaelthira prepared with trembling hands, the same cup that Kaelmor swept the floor around with devotional care, the same cup that has been filled and emptied and refilled countless times over the course of an era so vast that the era itself cannot be written. And I understand, with a clarity that makes my soul ache, that the being who holds that cup is the same being who has just reshaped the origin of his own existence while deliberately, consciously, absurdly restraining the scope of his intervention.

"Yes," I whisper. "I would like some tea."

Godric nods, his smile warm and genuine, and gestures for Vaelthira to bring another cup. And as I stand there, in the non-room of the imperial palace, at the right hand of the throne of the most powerful being in existence, I feel the weight of the realization settle over me like a shroud.

The King is holding back. The King has always been holding back. The King will always be holding back. And the held-back version is already so absurdly, ridiculously, incomprehensibly powerful that the concept of holding back is meaningless.

I take the cup from Vaelthira's trembling hands, and I drink the tea, and I try not to think about the recursive loop of infinite escalation that Godric has chosen not to initiate, and I fail.

The tea is cold. I do not care. The horror is warm enough.
